distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'Education is key to increased economic equality' and 'societies are not doing enough' as a general 'education needs reform' headline that includes higher education.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage never targets higher education or calls for urgent reform — it specifically recommends early-years investment. Step 3's paraphrase fixes the scope on PRESCHOOL, not universities.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'To get workers' paychecks up, they have to possess better skills' and read it as a description of progress already underway.
distractors.why_wrong: That sentence states a CONDITION (workers MUST have better skills), not an observation that they ARE getting them. The passage frames skilled workers as a goal, not a trend. Step 2 anchors this on the OECD's prescription, not an empirical claim.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that 'more money for education' sounds right, and 'equally on all kinds' feels like a fair, balanced position.
distractors.why_wrong: The pivot sentence explicitly says it ISN'T just more money — it's WHERE the money goes (early years specifically, not all education equally). D contradicts the passage's main qualifier.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-031.pitfall: When a paragraph contains a broad opening claim ('education is key') and a narrow specific recommendation ('invest in preschool'), distractors often promote the broad claim into the main point. The headline lives in the specific recommendation, not the framing sentence.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-031.solution_path: The 'Education and Pay' paragraph cites an OECD report recommending more investment in early years like preschool because that lift travels with a child through their whole career — that's the headline B captures.

steps.text: The prompt is 'What is the main point here?' — so you need the single idea that holds the whole 'Education and Pay' paragraph together, not a side detail. With main-point items, read the passage once and ask: if I had to title it in one sentence, what would I say?
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Two sentences carry the argument. The pivot one: 'Enhancing access to good education isn't just a matter of more money, however, but also how we spend that money.' The verdict one: the OECD 'recommended boosting investment in the early years of education, like preschool, since that improves a child's academic performance over his or her entire career.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Stripped down: education is the lever for economic equality, but pouring more money in only helps if it goes to the RIGHT stage — early years. Money invested at preschool keeps paying back across the student's whole school career.
steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English

steps.text: A few terms are worth pinning. 'Bolster' = strengthen, reinforce. 'Boost' = increase. 'Early years of education' = preschool / kindergarten, before formal schooling. 'Pay off later' = produce benefits in the future. These all support a 'spend early, reap later' reading.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A claims higher education needs urgent reform — the paragraph doesn't even mention higher education. B ('More money for the education of young children will pay off later on') matches the OECD recommendation almost word for word. C says skilled workers are increasing — the passage describes a problem (workers need better skills), not a trend. D ('More money equally on ALL kinds of education') contradicts the pivot sentence which says HOW we spend matters, not just how much.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. The OECD recommendation in the closing sentence is the paragraph's headline: investment in early-years education yields career-long returns.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-031.technique: On 'main point' items, find the sentence that names a PRESCRIPTION or PIVOT — usually starting with 'however', 'instead', or a research recommendation — and pick the option that paraphrases it. Skim for words like 'recommend', 'suggest', 'argue': they flag the author's headline.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to anchor on 'some scientists believe…; others believe this is impossible' and treat the existence of disagreement as the headline.
distractors.why_wrong: The scientific disagreement is the SETUP, not the conclusion. The passage uses 'regardless' as a hinge — meaning, set the scientific question aside; the philosophical question survives. Step 3 captures this hinge.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'philosophers are engaged in a debate' and assume the debate is for-or-against immortality.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage says they debate how immortality 'would CHANGE our humanity' — not whether it's good or bad. C imports a pro/con framing the text doesn't make. Step 2 anchors the actual debate topic.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a paragraph about competing predictions is that the writer is saying we can't predict the outcome.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage doesn't argue that prediction is impossible — it argues the philosophical work is worth doing regardless of which prediction wins. D collapses the 'whether or not' move into a 'we can't know' move, which is a different claim.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-032.pitfall: When a paragraph opens with 'some say X, others say Y', distractors often promote 'the disagreement' to be the main point. The author's real claim usually lies AFTER the disagreement, in the 'but here's why it matters' sentence.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-032.solution_path: The 'Immortality' paragraph notes that even if uploading minds into robots never works, the same philosophical questions arise from any 'human augmentation and improvements' — so the dilemmas surface whether or not eternal life is achieved. That's exactly what A says.

steps.text: The prompt is 'What is the main point in this text?' — so you need the central claim, not a side detail about who believes what. With main-point items, find the sentence that ties the passage together; everything else is scaffolding.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: The closing sentence carries the argument: 'Their dialogue is important because even if the final goal falls short, human augmentation and improvements may raise similar issues.' The middle one — 'legitimate philosophers are engaged in a debate over how such an eventuality would change our humanity' — sets up why the debate matters.
steps.title: Locate the key sentence

steps.text: Boiled down: scientists disagree about whether full immortality is reachable, but it doesn't matter for the philosophy — even partial steps (human augmentation, enhancements) will raise the same questions about what makes us human. So the debate is worthwhile either way.
steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English

steps.text: Worth glossing. 'Eventuality' = a possible future event. 'Falls short' = doesn't fully succeed. 'Augmentation' = enhancement, adding capabilities. The phrase 'whether eternal life is achieved or not' in option A directly mirrors 'even if the final goal falls short' in the passage.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says immortality-related dilemmas appear whether or not we reach eternal life — matches the closing 'even if the final goal falls short' clause exactly. B says it's questionable whether humans will become immortal — the passage mentions disagreement, but this isn't the main point; it's the setup. C claims philosophers quarrel about whether immortality is positive or negative — the passage says they debate how it would change humanity, not pro/con. D says predicting is impossible — the passage doesn't take that position; it sidesteps the prediction question entirely.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is A. The closing 'even if the final goal falls short' clause is the paragraph's whole reason for being — the dilemmas land either way, so the philosophical work is worth doing now.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-032.technique: On 'main point' items in short paragraphs, scan for hinge words like 'regardless', 'either way', 'whether or not', 'even if' — those signal the author moving past a setup into the actual claim. The correct option almost always mirrors that hinge.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'something changed the way humans think' as 'we don't know what caused it' and treat the question as a mystery.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage isn't presenting a mystery — it's presenting two specific candidate explanations and weighing them. Step 2 shows both are named explicitly: population growth vs. affluence.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'moral religions' and assume the paragraph is making a universal claim about religion and morality being linked.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph isn't claiming morality is the foundation of all religion — it's investigating what triggered SPECIFICALLY the moral religions to emerge. The scope is much narrower than B suggests.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is that 'moral religion' implies morality flows FROM religion, so D's 'morality as a side effect of religion' sounds intuitive.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph runs the arrow the other way — it asks what CAUSED moral religions to arise, not what religions caused. Both candidate explanations (population, affluence) sit BEFORE the religions emerged. Step 3 fixes the direction.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-033.pitfall: Scope traps are the main hazard here: distractors often paraphrase a sentence that appears LATER in the article, not in the opening paragraph. Anchor on what the opening paragraph alone establishes.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-033.solution_path: The opening paragraph of 'The Rise of Modern Religions' sets up a contrast: scholars have long attributed moral religion to population growth, but a recent study points to rising affluence instead. That competing-views framing is exactly what C names.

steps.text: The prompt asks 'What is the main point in the OPENING paragraph?' — so you only care about the first paragraph, not the whole article. Re-read just that paragraph and ask: what's the headline of that block?
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Two clauses carry the argument. The traditional view: 'Scholars often attribute the rise of these moral religions… to population growth, seeing morality as a necessary social stabilizer.' The challenger: 'Yet findings from a recent study published in Current Biology point to a different factor: rising affluence.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Stripped down: there's a long-standing explanation (moral religions arose because growing populations needed a social glue) and a new one (they arose because people got rich enough to think about morality). The opening paragraph stages those two explanations side by side.
steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English

steps.text: Worth pinning. 'Attribute X to Y' = explain X by saying Y caused it. 'Stabilizer' = something that keeps a system from breaking down. 'Affluence' = wealth, prosperity. The pivot 'Yet' between the two explanations is the structural hinge.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A claims origins have been 'a mystery' — the passage says the opposite: scholars HAVE long had an explanation (population growth), it's just being challenged. B claims moral values underpin all religious belief — the passage doesn't make that universal claim. C ('competing views on the emergence of moral religions') is a clean match for the population-vs-affluence contrast. D claims morality is a side effect of religious thinking — the passage runs the causality the other way (something gives rise TO religion).
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is C. The opening paragraph's whole structure is 'scholars say X, but a new study says Y' — two competing views on the same question. That's the headline.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-033.technique: On 'opening paragraph' main-point items, look for the structural move 'X has been said, BUT now Y'. The correct option usually names that contrast — either 'competing views' or 'a new challenge to the old view'. Don't extrapolate beyond the first paragraph.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to hear 'morality and asceticism' on one side and 'affluence' on the other and read the study as setting up a tension between wealth and moral restraint.
distractors.why_wrong: The study argues affluence ENABLED morality, not that it conflicted with it. Step 3's paraphrase fixes the relationship as causal, not adversarial.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'population growth' in the first paragraph and assume the study uses population statistics in some surprising way.
distractors.why_wrong: Population growth is the OLD scholarly view; the new study uses energy / calorie statistics instead. C confuses what the study contains with what it replaces. Step 2 anchors the actual study findings.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a study that 'points to a different factor' is that the study openly attacks the old view.
distractors.why_wrong: The text uses 'a different factor' — neutral, not critical. Baumard offers an alternative, not a polemic. The critical voices in the article come from OTHER researchers (King, Crespi) responding to Baumard, not Baumard attacking previous work.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-034.pitfall: Don't confuse the study's claim with the article's framing of the study. The article narrates a controversy; the study itself just makes one claim (energy threshold → moral religion). Anchor on that single claim.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-034.solution_path: The new study identifies access to roughly 20,000 calories of energy per day — food, fuel, raw materials — as the threshold for moral religion to emerge. Material conditions, not population pressure, are the trigger. That's what B captures.

steps.text: The prompt asks 'What is said about the new study mentioned?' — so you need the study's central finding, not the surrounding debate. Locate the study description and ask: what does the study claim is the cause?
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: The second paragraph spells it out: 'Affluence emerged as a major force in the rise of moral religion — in particular, access to energy. Across cultures, moral religions abruptly emerged when members of a population could reliably source 20,000 calories of energy a day, including food (for humans and livestock), fuel and raw materials.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: the study found a specific threshold — about 20,000 daily calories of usable energy — at which point moral religions emerged. The trigger isn't crowding (population), it's having enough physical resources (food, fuel, raw materials).
steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English

steps.text: Worth glossing. 'Material conditions' = the physical / economic stuff people have access to (food, energy, goods) — the OPPOSITE of mental or spiritual conditions. 'Affluence' = wealth. The phrase 'material conditions' in option B is a direct paraphrase of 'food, fuel and raw materials'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says the argument is built on a CONFLICT between wealth and morality — but the study claims wealth CAUSED morality, they aren't in conflict. B ('stresses the relevance of material conditions for the appearance of modern religions') is a clean paraphrase: material conditions = energy + food + raw materials; appearance = emergence. C claims population statistics surprised researchers — population is the OLD view the study is replacing, not the study's own use. D claims the study is openly critical of previous attempts — the writer presents the study as an alternative explanation, not as a critique.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. The study's central claim is a material threshold — 20,000 calories of energy a day — preceded moral religion. Material conditions, in plain English.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-034.technique: On 'what is said about the study' items, look for the study's own positive claim — usually a specific number, threshold, or causal mechanism — and pick the option that paraphrases it in abstract terms. Distractors typically borrow vocabulary from the surrounding debate, not the study itself.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read King's emphasis on 'deep roots of compassion and morality in other primates' as her saying Baumard hadn't considered pre-religious morality at all.
distractors.why_wrong: Baumard explicitly does acknowledge pre-existing moral qualities ('probably existed in humans before'). King's critique is that Baumard underweights this, not that he's blind to it. Step 5 anchors on the exact passage.

distractors.why_tempting: Many readers, scanning for an objection, assume King attacks the central evidence — Baumard's calorie statistics.
distractors.why_wrong: King actually accepts the calorie idea — she calls it a 'nudge'. Her objection is the abruptness of the transition, not the truth of the energy threshold. Step 3 makes this concession explicit.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'King says X about Baumard' is that X might be supportive — she does mention primate research after all.
distractors.why_wrong: King uses primate research to UNDERMINE Baumard's abruptness, not to support him. The primate finding suggests morality predates the calorie threshold, which weakens Baumard's emergence-by-affluence story. C inverts the function of King's evidence.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-035.pitfall: Don't confuse a critic's evidence (King's primate citation) with their core objection (abruptness). Distractors often promote the supporting evidence into the headline; the actual claim is usually in the verb of the critique.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-035.solution_path: Barbara King argues the study 'exaggerates the sharp transition' to moral belief systems and suggests a more gradual change instead — she's pushing back on how ABRUPT Baumard's emergence story is. That's option D.

steps.text: The prompt is 'How does Barbara King regard Nicolas Baumard's research?' — so you only care about King's specific critique, not the wider debate. Find King's quoted block and ask: what's her one objection?
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: King's block: 'the study exaggerates the sharp transition to the moral belief systems. She suggests that a more gradual transition may have taken place — one that was perhaps nudged over the line by a reliable calorie count.' And: 'I don't see any reason to assume that cosmological morality and compassion were not important to earlier hunter-gatherer groups.'
steps.title: Locate King's argument

steps.text: In plain English: King doesn't reject the calorie story — she even calls calories a 'nudge'. Her issue is the SHAPE of the transition. Baumard treats moral religion as appearing suddenly when the threshold is crossed; King thinks morality was already developing gradually and the threshold just nudged it forward.
steps.title: Paraphrase King's view

steps.text: Worth pinning. 'Abruptness' = the quality of happening suddenly. 'Exaggerates' = overstates, makes seem stronger than it is. 'Gradual' = slow, step-by-step. Option D's 'overstates the abruptness' is a direct rewording of 'exaggerates the sharp transition'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A claims Baumard is UNAWARE of earlier moral thinking — but Baumard explicitly acknowledges moral qualities pre-existed: 'moral and ascetic qualities probably existed in humans before the major religions emphasized them.' So unawareness is too strong. B claims King attacks Baumard's calorie statistics — she doesn't; she actually accepts the calorie nudge idea. C claims King says other branches of research support Baumard — the OPPOSITE: she cites primate research that complicates his story. D ('Baumard overstates the abruptness of the appearance of morality') = 'exaggerates the sharp transition' = correct.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is D. King's specific quarrel is with the SUDDENNESS of Baumard's story — she thinks moral capacity was already brewing in earlier human and pre-human populations, so the calorie threshold was a tipping point, not a starting point.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-035.technique: On 'how does X regard the research' items, find the quoted critic's ONE specific objection — usually one sentence with words like 'exaggerates', 'overstates', 'underestimates', 'reverses'. The correct option mirrors that single objection in abstract terms.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'the main idea is fascinating' as praise and conclude Crespi mostly accepts Baumard's case, with minor caveats.
distractors.why_wrong: Suggesting the causality might be REVERSED isn't a minor reservation — it's a fundamental challenge to the study's claim. Step 3's paraphrase shows the depth of his pushback.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'fascinating' and assume Crespi endorses Baumard's affluence-as-cause argument.
distractors.why_wrong: Crespi explicitly suggests religion might cause affluence, not the other way round. C inverts his position; he questions affluence's role, not affirms it. Step 2 anchors on his actual quote.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a critic raising a 'but' is that they find the data weak or beside the point.
distractors.why_wrong: Crespi's objection isn't about statistics or relevance — it's about CAUSAL DIRECTION. He doesn't argue the data is irrelevant; he argues it's compatible with the opposite causal story. Step 5 makes the distinction explicit.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-036.pitfall: A polite opener ('fascinating idea') can fool you into reading the critic as mostly supportive. The objection's WEIGHT is in the substance after the 'but', not the friendliness before it.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-036.solution_path: Crespi praises the IDEA but says the causal link between affluence and religion isn't yet established — and even hints the arrow may run the other way (religion → affluence). His objection is that Baumard's evidence isn't strong enough yet, which is what A names.

steps.text: The prompt asks 'How does Bernard J. Crespi view Nicolas Baumard's findings?' — so you only care about Crespi's specific reaction, not the broader debate. Find Crespi's quoted block and ask: what's his one objection?
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Crespi's quote: 'The main idea in the article is fascinating, but the causal link between increasing affluence and religion remains to be established. Our work actually suggests that the authors might have their causality reversed — that religion itself drives increases in affluence via its effects on increased cooperation.'
steps.title: Locate Crespi's argument

steps.text: In plain English: Crespi likes the hypothesis but says Baumard hasn't proven the cause-and-effect direction. He even suggests the arrow could run backward — religion creating affluence, not the other way around. Either way, Baumard's evidence doesn't yet pin down the causality.
steps.title: Paraphrase Crespi's view

steps.text: Worth pinning. 'Insufficient' = not enough. 'Causal link' = the chain showing X causes Y. 'Remains to be established' = still has to be proven. Option A's 'evidence for affluence giving rise to religion is insufficient' is a direct rewording of 'the causal link… remains to be established'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A ('evidence for affluence giving rise to religion is insufficient') = 'causal link remains to be established' = correct. B claims Crespi accepts the account with minor reservations — but 'causality might be reversed' is a fundamental objection, not minor. C claims Crespi agrees affluence's role is crucial — he actually questions whether affluence drives religion at all. D claims Crespi finds the statistics interesting but irrelevant — he calls the IDEA fascinating, not the statistics, and his issue is causality, not relevance.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is A. Crespi's politeness ('fascinating idea') is a wrapper around a real critique: Baumard hasn't shown which way the arrow points. That's the textbook 'insufficient evidence' objection.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-036.technique: On 'how does X view Y' items where the critic opens with praise ('fascinating', 'interesting', 'thoughtful'), ignore the pleasantry and focus on the conjunction that follows ('but', 'however', 'yet'). The real objection lives after the conjunction.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to count the critics' voices (King and Crespi both push back) and conclude the writer is conveying serious objections.
distractors.why_wrong: The objections are King's and Crespi's, not the writer's own. The writer reports them neutrally and ends on a line that preserves Baumard's claim ('morality… takes energy'). Step 2 separates the reporter's voice from the sources'.

distractors.why_tempting: Many readers see Crespi raise the strongest objection and assume the writer must be even more skeptical than Crespi.
distractors.why_wrong: The closing line is sympathetic to Baumard, not more skeptical than Crespi. Crespi suggests reversed causality; the writer's closing keeps the energy-morality association alive 'whether cause or effect'. Step 3 shows the writer remains in Baumard's camp.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct is to read King's measured objection as the moderate position and assume the writer matches it.
distractors.why_wrong: King thinks the transition was gradual; the writer doesn't engage with that specific claim. King is more reserved than the writer, not less. C's 'avoids taking a stand on a key issue' captures the writer's actual hedge — the causality question — which King doesn't raise.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-037.pitfall: Don't conflate the voices in a sourced article. Critics speak in quotes; the writer speaks in the connective tissue. The question asks about the writer, so anchor on intros and closings, not the quoted disputes.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-037.solution_path: The writer presents Baumard's case with enthusiasm and gives critics fair airtime, then closes with 'whether cause or effect, morality, it seems, takes energy' — clearly impressed by the link, but consciously refusing to settle the causality question Crespi raised. That's exactly C.

steps.text: The prompt asks for the WRITER's overall attitude — not Baumard's, not King's, not Crespi's. So you need to read the framing language (introductions, transitions, the closing) where the writer's voice is most visible.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: Three sections give the writer away. The intro frames Baumard's idea as substantive ('findings from a recent study… point to a different factor'). The middle gives King and Crespi balanced space. The closing: 'Whether cause or effect, morality, it seems, takes energy.' That last sentence is the writer dodging the direction-of-causality question.
steps.title: Locate the writer's voice

steps.text: In plain English: the writer is clearly drawn to the affluence-energy link — it gets prime real estate and a memorable closing line. But on the specific question Crespi raised (does affluence cause religion, or vice versa?), the writer hedges with 'whether cause or effect'. They like the association but won't claim the direction.
steps.title: Paraphrase the writer's stance

steps.text: Worth pinning. 'Impressed' = approving, taken with. 'Take a stand' = commit to one side of a debate. 'Key issue' = the central question. The closing's 'whether cause or effect' is the textbook 'I'm not taking a stand' move on a debated point.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A ('positive but with serious objections') — the writer doesn't voice their own serious objections; they let King and Crespi raise them. B (less convinced than Crespi) — Crespi is actually the more skeptical voice; the writer ends with the energy-morality link intact. C ('clearly impressed but avoids taking a stand on a key issue') — matches: the closing line praises the link but ducks the causality question. D (more sceptical than King) — King is the more measured skeptic; the writer is friendlier to Baumard than that.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is C. The writer's voice across the piece is admiring of the Baumard finding, but the deliberate hedge in the final sentence — 'whether cause or effect' — explicitly refuses to settle Crespi's challenge.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-037.technique: On 'writer's overall attitude' items, the framing language (intros, transitions, closings) carries the verdict — not the quoted critics. Look for hedging phrases like 'whether X or Y', 'remains an open question', 'it seems': those signal the writer ducking a specific debate while still endorsing the broader idea.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'voice can provide emotional cues' as a claim about how autistic people EXPRESS themselves vocally, especially if you skim past 'when listening to voices'.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage is about how autistic people PERCEIVE emotional cues in OTHERS' voices, not how they express their own emotions. Step 3 fixes the direction: receiving, not transmitting.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'as well as — or even better than — neurotypical people' and read it as a sweeping claim that autistic people generally outperform neurotypical people.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage carefully qualifies: 'at least for some subgroups of autistic people in certain situations'. The general claim in C ignores those qualifiers and overshoots the data.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a paragraph mentioning 'treatment perspective' is to read it as a comparison of how treatable each kind of deficit is.
distractors.why_wrong: Pelphrey is contrasting treating face-reading vs. treating a fundamental ALL-modality deficit — not face-deficits vs. voice-deficits. D imports a comparison the passage doesn't make. Step 5 separates the actual contrast from D's invented one.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-038.pitfall: When a paragraph contains a striking claim ('better than neurotypical people'), distractors often promote it into a sweeping rule. The author's actual headline usually keeps the careful qualifiers (subgroups, certain situations) intact.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-038.solution_path: The 'Autism' paragraph notes that people with autism struggle to read faces but can recognize emotion through voices, so the deficit may be confined PRIMARILY to vision. That's exactly B — their feeling-recognition trouble relates to what can be seen.

steps.text: The prompt asks for the main point 'in relation to autistic people' — so you need the central claim about autism specifically, not a side comment about treatment. Find the sentence that frames what's new about the research.
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: The pivot sentence: 'Yet the voice, too, can provide emotional cues, and several recent studies indicate that when listening to voices, people with autism can actually recognize feelings and other traits of humanness as well as — or even better than — neurotypical people do.' Verdict sentence: 'deficits in identifying emotions could be confined primarily to vision.'
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: the old assumption was that autism means a broad emotion-reading deficit. The new finding flips this — people with autism CAN read emotion from voices, sometimes even better than neurotypical people. So the trouble seems to be specifically with FACES (vision), not with emotion in general.
steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English

steps.text: Worth pinning. 'Confined to vision' = limited to what is seen visually. 'Modalities' = different channels of sensing (sight, sound, etc.). The phrase 'what can be seen' in option B is a direct match for 'confined primarily to vision'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A says autistic people EXPRESS emotion more through voice — but the passage is about RECEIVING emotional cues (recognizing other people's feelings), not expressing their own. B ('difficulties in distinguishing feelings may relate to what can be seen') matches the closing 'deficits… confined primarily to vision' exactly. C says they're generally better than neurotypical people — the passage qualifies this ('at least for some subgroups… in certain situations'), so the strong universal claim overshoots. D claims visual deficits are easier to treat than aural deficits — Pelphrey says treating face-reading is easier than treating a 'fundamental lack of understanding of emotion from ALL modalities', which isn't a face-vs-voice comparison.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. The headline finding is that autism's emotion-reading trouble is largely SPECIFIC to vision — auditory emotion recognition can be intact or even superior. Vision = what can be seen, which is what B says.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-038.technique: On 'main point about X' items, find the sentence that names a SHIFT or QUALIFIER ('yet', 'however', 'actually') reframing what was previously assumed about X. The correct option almost always paraphrases that shift in abstract terms.

distractors.why_tempting: If you skim and lock onto a single salient theme (e.g., 'all about manufacturing' or 'all about tech'), one-dimensional sounds plausible.
distractors.why_wrong: The list explicitly crosses tech, finance, infrastructure, art, energy, and social policy — that's the opposite of one-dimensional. Step 2's full list rules it out.

distractors.why_tempting: Many readers latch on 'solar and wind industry' and 'methanol as an alternative fuel' and read the paragraph as a green-tech showcase.
distractors.why_wrong: Solar / wind / methanol are three items in a list of thirteen; the same list also includes car manufacture and electronics. Step 3 anchors on the breadth, not on one slice of it.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'genetic engineering' applied to humans is to read it as ethically charged.
distractors.why_wrong: The writer describes the genetic engineering matter-of-factly, alongside neutral items like rail and luxury goods. There's no moral framing — D imports an ethical judgment the text doesn't make.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-039.pitfall: Distractors will often pick up one item from a long list and inflate it into the headline (solar = environment-friendly; millionaires = unethical). The correct option matches the SHAPE of the list, not one of its entries.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-039.solution_path: The 'China Today' paragraph lists a dizzyingly mixed bag of areas where China leads — genetic engineering, mobile banking, luxury goods, rail, manufacturing, solar, wind, methanol, millionaires, poverty reduction. That breadth across so many unrelated domains is exactly what 'extremely varied' captures. Answer B.

steps.text: The prompt asks which phrase best characterizes China's OVERALL progress 'as described in the text'. So you need to take the whole list and ask: if you had to summarize the SHAPE of the progress in one adjective, what would it be?
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: The list is the whole paragraph: 'genetic engineering', 'mobile banking', 'luxury goods', 'high-speed rail', 'car manufacture', 'foreign trade', 'electronic products', 'art market', 'broadband subscribers', 'solar and wind industry', 'methanol as alternative fuel', 'growth of millionaires', 'poverty reduction'. Note the last pair: millionaires AND poverty reduction — those pull in opposite directions.
steps.title: Locate the key list

steps.text: Boiled down: the writer isn't picking one theme. They're piling up domains as different as bullet trains, art, solar power, and luxury — including paired opposites (luxury / poverty reduction). The whole rhetorical effect is 'leading in everything, across the board'.
steps.title: Paraphrase the shape of the list

steps.text: Worth pinning. 'One-dimensional' = having only one side, narrow. 'Extremely varied' = covering many different things. 'Environment-friendly' = good for the environment. 'Unethical' = morally wrong. Reading the list, the only adjective that captures 'genetic engineering AND luxury AND poverty reduction AND solar' simultaneously is 'varied'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A 'one-dimensional' — the opposite of what the list shows; the list spans more than a dozen unrelated domains. B 'extremely varied' — exactly fits a list that includes everything from genetic engineering to poverty reduction. C 'environment-friendly' — solar and wind get mentioned, but so do car manufacturing and electronics; the list is mixed, not green. D 'basically unethical' — the text doesn't make any moral judgment about China's leads; 'genetic engineering' might raise eyebrows, but the writer doesn't frame anything as unethical.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. The list's whole rhetorical move is to span unrelated domains — and the closing 'growth of millionaires AND poverty reduction' juxtaposes two opposite-direction trends in one breath. Varied is the only adjective that holds it together.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-039.technique: On 'best characterizes the overall progress' items, count the DOMAINS in the list. If they cross multiple unrelated fields, 'varied' or 'broad' is almost always right. If they cluster around one theme, look for an adjective naming that theme.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to assume that any article about a 'new disorder' is going to be about the slow medical-system process of getting it recognized.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph documents the opposite — recognition was FAST, driven by aggressive PR rather than blocked by bureaucracy. Step 3 anchors on Glaxo Smith Kline as the accelerator, not on any bureaucratic obstacle.

distractors.why_tempting: Many readers catch 'company-funded, as yet unpublished study' and assume the paragraph is debunking inflated prevalence statistics.
distractors.why_wrong: The paragraph doesn't actually compare RLS prevalence to other estimates — it questions WHO is doing the publicizing, not whether the numbers are inflated. The headline is about the funding behind the awareness, not the size of the disorder.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on a paragraph mentioning a pharmaceutical company is that it's about drug development economics.
distractors.why_wrong: Glaxo Smith Kline's campaign was about CREATING DEMAND for awareness (and presumably treatment), not about delays in PRODUCTION. D names a totally different pharma-industry story. Step 5 fixes the focus on marketing, not manufacturing.

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-040.pitfall: The 'nonprofit' RLS Foundation revelation is the rhetorical kicker — and easy to misread as the headline. The actual headline is what the case ILLUSTRATES: commercial interests driving lesser-known-disorder publicity.
host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-040.solution_path: The 'Restless Legs Syndrome' paragraph traces how Glaxo Smith Kline mounted a press-release campaign and funds the supposedly 'nonprofit' RLS Foundation — the awareness-raising about this lesser-known disorder is being driven by commercial interests, exactly what B says.

steps.text: The prompt is 'What is the main point here?' — so you need the single idea holding the 'Restless Legs Syndrome' paragraph together. Read it once and ask: what is the writer's takeaway about this awareness campaign?
steps.title: Understand the question

steps.text: The paragraph's structural backbone: Glaxo Smith Kline made restless legs syndrome a 'household term' through 'a vigorous awareness campaign'. The campaign relied on company-issued press releases and a 'company-funded, as yet unpublished study'. The closing twist: the 'nonprofit Restless Legs Syndrome Foundation' that the press releases steered readers to is 'heavily subsidized by Glaxo Smith Kline'.
steps.title: Locate the key sentences

steps.text: Boiled down: the public 'discovered' restless legs syndrome because a drug company spent money making it news. Even the foundation that supposedly helps patients is bankrolled by that drug company. So the 'awareness' is really marketing — commercial interests dressed up as health information.
steps.title: Paraphrase in plain English

steps.text: Worth pinning. 'Spread the word about' = raise awareness, publicize. 'Lesser-known disorder' = a condition the public knows little about — exactly what RLS was before 2003. 'Commercial interests' = profit motives. Option B's three pieces — 'spread the word', 'lesser-known disorder', 'commercial interests' — all map directly onto the paragraph.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Walk each option. A claims recognition of new disorders is slowed by BUREAUCRATIC obstacles — but the paragraph shows the OPPOSITE: a pharma company speeding recognition through PR. B ('Attempts to spread the word about lesser-known disorders may be due to commercial interests') matches the GSK case beat for beat. C says RLS is less widespread than studies have claimed — the paragraph hints the studies are company-funded, but the headline isn't about prevalence, it's about who's driving the awareness. D says medication production is delayed by lack of incentives — irrelevant; the paragraph is about marketing, not production delays.
steps.title: Match against the options

steps.text: The answer is B. The paragraph is a case study in how a pharma company manufactured public awareness of a lesser-known condition — using press releases, a company-funded study, and a nominally-independent foundation it actually subsidizes. Commercial interests, not health advocacy, were the engine.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb1-ELF-040.technique: On 'main point' items where a paragraph builds a case study of one organization's behavior, the headline is almost always the ABSTRACT pattern that case illustrates ('commercial interests behind awareness campaigns'), not the specific facts. Read the option for the generalisation; check the paragraph for the example.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'A new study' two sentences earlier and assume 'new' echoes back to the preferences themselves.
distractors.why_wrong: The study is new; the preference it describes is centuries old. The whole point of the passage is that the preference is unchanged. Step 3 fixes the direction: old, not new.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at the strong word 'Wrenching changes' and assume the writer is editorializing — treating women's preferences as irrational in a modern economy.
distractors.why_wrong: The writer takes no normative stance on the preference; they just report that it has persisted while the labour market has changed. 'Illogical' would be the writer judging the preference, which the text never does.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'wanting a man with money' as a modern preference is that it can feel out-of-step or peculiar today.
distractors.why_wrong: The preference is the MAJORITY view (78% of never-married women) — that's the opposite of 'odd'. Step 5's plug test rules out 'odd' on the basis of 'this is the dominant pattern, not a strange one'.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-031.pitfall: Cloze adjective options often include a strong evaluative word ('illogical', 'odd') that imports a stance the writer never takes. Default to the descriptive adjective unless the writer is clearly editorialising.
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-031.solution_path: Gap 31 sits in 'Wrenching changes in the labour market, combined with these ___ preferences, have shaken up the marriage market.' The 'these preferences' refers back to women's age-old wish for a husband with money — a preference traced back to Jane Austen's 19th-century characters. So the slot wants 'ancient'. Answer C.

steps.text: This is a gap-fill (cloze) adjective question — pick the word that best fits the blank. The slot is modifying 'preferences', so you need to figure out which TYPE of preferences the writer means: novel ones, irrational ones, long-standing ones, or strange ones.
steps.title: Understand the gap type

steps.text: Read what's around the gap. The preceding setup names a preference that goes back to Jane Austen's 19th-century characters: 'men with money made more eligible mates'. Then a 'new study' confirms 'little has changed' — 78% of never-married American women still want a steady-job spouse. The gap then says 'Wrenching changes in the labour market, combined with these ___ preferences, have shaken up the marriage market.'
steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap

steps.text: The preferences in question — wanting a financially stable husband — are explicitly traced back two centuries and described as unchanged. So the writer is signalling: 'these preferences are OLD, going back a long way'. The adjective needed expresses long-standing, traditional, going-back-centuries.
steps.title: Identify the relationship needed

steps.text: Gloss the candidates. 'New' = recent, freshly arisen. 'Illogical' = not rational. 'Ancient' = very old, going back centuries. 'Odd' = strange, peculiar. Only 'ancient' matches a preference traced back to Austen's 19th-century setting (and presumably earlier).
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Plug each option into the slot and test. 'These NEW preferences' — contradicts the passage, which says 'little has changed'. 'These ILLOGICAL preferences' — the writer takes no rational/irrational stance on the preference, just notes its persistence. 'These ANCIENT preferences' — fits perfectly: a preference dating to Austen's era is exactly that. 'These ODD preferences' — the writer doesn't frame the preference as strange; it's the majority view of American women.
steps.title: Plug and test each option

steps.text: The answer is C. The slot needs a word that captures 'old, going back to 19th-century characters'. Only 'ancient' does that work.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-031.technique: On cloze adjectives modifying a noun like 'preferences' or 'attitudes', check whether the surrounding passage establishes the noun as recent, persistent, irrational, or rare. The adjective must match what the passage has ALREADY characterised about the noun.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to associate 'marriage market' with women being financially dependent on men, and read 'their dependence' as something men might court.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Vie for dependence' isn't an English collocation — you don't compete for someone TO depend on you. The relationship here is about being CHOSEN, not about creating dependence. Step 4 rules this out as ungrammatical.

distractors.why_tempting: Many readers, scanning for a relationship-related word, latch on to 'relation' as the closest match.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Vie for their relation' is grammatically wrong; the word you'd need is 'relationship' or 'company'. 'Their relation' could mean 'their relative' (a family member), which is nonsensical here. Step 5's plug test makes the awkwardness audible.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct, given the passage is about men 'with steady jobs' attracting women, is that money / income is in play.
distractors.why_wrong: The passage runs the money the OTHER way — women want men WITH income, not men chasing women's income. D inverts the courtship direction. Step 3 fixes who's competing for what.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-032.pitfall: When 'vie for' or 'compete for' is the verb, fixed English idioms usually decide the slot before context does. Don't import a word that needs a different preposition or article to work ('a relationship', not 'their relation').
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-032.solution_path: Gap 32 sits in 'there were 139 young, never-married men vying for their ___.' Men 'vying for' young women in a 1960 marriage market are competing for the women's attention — that's the natural collocation with 'vie for'. Answer A.

steps.text: This is a gap-fill (cloze) noun question — what are the men competing for? The slot is the OBJECT of 'vying for', so think about what people typically 'vie for' in a courtship/marriage context.
steps.title: Understand the gap type

steps.text: The sentence: 'In 1960, young, never-married women were spoilt for choice. For every hundred of them aged 25–34, there were 139 young, never-married men vying for their ___.' Surrounding context: a marriage market where 139 men compete for every 100 women.
steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap

steps.text: The set-up is a courtship surplus — more men than women available for marriage. What men in that situation try to win from women is the women's notice, their interest, their favourable consideration. The relationship needed is 'object of a courtship competition'.
steps.title: Identify the relationship needed

steps.text: Gloss the candidates. 'Vie for attention' = compete for someone's notice — a fixed English collocation, especially in courtship. 'Vie for dependence' isn't an English collocation at all. 'Vie for relation' is grammatically awkward; you'd say 'a relationship', not 'their relation'. 'Vie for income' would mean competing for the women's money — irrelevant to a courtship surplus.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Plug each option into the slot. 'Vying for their ATTENTION' — natural English; men competing to be noticed by women. 'Vying for their DEPENDENCE' — odd; you don't vie for someone to depend on you. 'Vying for their RELATION' — ungrammatical with 'relation' (you'd say 'their friendship' or 'a relationship'). 'Vying for their INCOME' — the passage is about men with money pursuing women, not about pursuing women's money.
steps.title: Plug and test each option

steps.text: The answer is A. 'Vie for attention' is the standard English collocation for competing for someone's notice, and in a 1960 marriage market with more men than women, attention is exactly what was scarce on the men's side.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-032.technique: On cloze nouns following a verb like 'vie for', 'compete for', 'fight for', think in terms of fixed English collocations first. 'Vie for attention' and 'vie for power' are the standard pairings; if no fixed collocation fits, then think about the situation's logic.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'more likely to say' as a survey claim and 'deserve marriage' as a kind of self-affirmation people might report.
distractors.why_wrong: People don't typically describe themselves as 'deserving' an institution in surveys — they describe themselves as valuing or wanting it. 'Deserve' is awkward, and it doesn't produce the right contrast with the low actual marriage rate. Step 4 rules it out on idiom grounds.

distractors.why_tempting: Many stop at 'although' and look for any neutral-to-positive verb — 'accept marriage' sounds vaguely positive without being too strong.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Accept' is passive — you accept something you didn't seek. The 'more likely than other races' wording implies an ACTIVE positive endorsement, not a shrugging acceptance. Step 5's plug test exposes how thin 'accept' is.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'only 26% are actually married' is that the slot must explain WHY rates are so low — maybe by saying they disregard marriage.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Disregard' would make the sentence consistent, not contradictory — and the 'although' demands a contradiction. If they disregarded marriage, the low rate wouldn't need explaining; it would follow naturally. Step 3 anchors on the 'although' as a contrast signal.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-033.pitfall: Concession words ('although', 'though') invert what you'd expect from the surface meaning. Always identify the second clause's direction first, then pick a slot word that points OPPOSITE.
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-033.solution_path: Gap 33 sits in 'African-Americans are more likely than other races to say they ___ marriage, only 26% of black women are actually married'. The 'although… only 26% are actually married' frames a contradiction, so the gap needs the OPPOSITE of low marriage rates — a positive attitude. 'Value' fits perfectly. Answer D.

steps.text: This is a gap-fill (cloze) verb question — what attitude do African-Americans have toward marriage according to surveys? The slot is the verb whose object is 'marriage'.
steps.title: Understand the gap type

steps.text: Full sentence: 'This helps explain why, although African-Americans are more likely than other races to say they ___ marriage, only 26% of black women are actually married, compared with 51% of whites.' Note the 'although'.
steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap

steps.text: The 'although… only 26% are married' signals a CONTRAST. What follows 'although' must be the EXPECTED-positive attitude that the low marriage rate contradicts. So the slot needs a verb expressing a positive attitude toward marriage: cherish, prize, value, endorse. The mismatch is between WANTING marriage and actually MARRYING.
steps.title: Identify the relationship needed

steps.text: Gloss the candidates. 'Deserve marriage' = be worthy of marriage — odd; people don't typically describe themselves as 'deserving' marriage. 'Accept marriage' = grudgingly allow it — too passive for the contrast with 'more likely than other races'. 'Disregard marriage' = ignore it — the OPPOSITE of what the contrast needs. 'Value marriage' = consider it important — clean, idiomatic, and fits the positive-attitude requirement.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Plug each option into the slot. 'Say they DESERVE marriage' — would imply moral entitlement, an odd thing to claim in a survey. 'Say they ACCEPT marriage' — accepting is a passive shrug, not the kind of attitude you'd be MORE LIKELY than others to express. 'Say they DISREGARD marriage' — collapses the 'although' contrast entirely; if they disregarded marriage, low marriage rates would be CONSISTENT, not contradictory. 'Say they VALUE marriage' — fits the 'although they value it, only 26% are married' contrast perfectly.
steps.title: Plug and test each option

steps.text: The answer is D. The 'although' demands a POSITIVE attitude in the first half that the low marriage rate contradicts. Only 'value marriage' produces the right contrast and matches the natural English idiom.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-033.technique: On cloze verbs where the sentence uses 'although', 'yet', 'however', or 'despite', the slot must produce a CONTRAST with the second clause. Plug in each option and check whether the resulting sentence sets up a real surprise — distractors usually collapse the contrast.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read the gap as a 'paradox' connector — '118 men per 100 women, ALTHOUGH women still find it hard' — and reach for the contrast word.
distractors.why_wrong: The two clauses aren't in contrast; clause 2 EXPLAINS clause 1, not contradicts it. The whole point is that women marrying older partners CAUSES the young-male surplus. Step 3 fixes the logical relationship as causal, not contrastive.

distractors.why_tempting: Many readers, when conjunctions appear, grab 'unless' as a hedge — 'this ratio holds, unless something else is going on'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Unless' would make the whole statement collapse — the ratio is a measured fact, not a contingency. Step 5 exposes the absurdity: you can't say 'there ARE 118 men, unless women marry older'. The ratio just IS.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct, given the chain of preferences in the passage, is that 'provided' (= on the condition that) might frame women's preference as the prerequisite for the surplus.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Provided' marks a future condition, not an existing cause. The passage describes a current statistical reality: 118:100 NOW, BECAUSE women marry older partners NOW. Step 3 distinguishes 'because' (already true) from 'provided' (hypothetical condition).

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-034.pitfall: The four cloze conjunction options are designed to be sentence-level synonyms in isolation but signal completely different logics. Don't pick based on which 'sounds smoother' — identify the relationship first, then match.
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-034.solution_path: Gap 34 sits between two clauses: 'There are 118 unmarried 25-year-old men for every 100 single women' and 'women are more likely to marry older partners'. The second clause EXPLAINS why the first ratio doesn't translate into easy mate-finding for 25-year-old men. 'Since' (= 'because') is the conjunction for an explanation. Answer C.

steps.text: This is a gap-fill (cloze) conjunction question — what's the logical relationship between the two clauses? Read both clauses and ask: is the second a contrast, a condition, a cause, or a consequence?
steps.title: Understand the gap type

steps.text: Clause 1: 'There are 118 unmarried 25-year-old men for every 100 single women' — a surplus of young men. Clause 2: 'women are more likely to marry older partners' — women's mating choices reach upward in age. Surrounding context (next sentences): the surplus closes by age 40 and inverts by 64, exactly because the women in question marry older men, removing them from the 25-year-old pool.
steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap

steps.text: Clause 2 isn't a contrast with clause 1 — it doesn't contradict the 118:100 ratio. It's the EXPLANATION for why that ratio exists at age 25: young women aren't matched with same-age men because they're marrying older ones. So the gap needs a CAUSAL connector — 'since', 'because', 'as'.
steps.title: Identify the relationship needed

steps.text: Gloss the candidates. 'Although' = in spite of — signals CONTRAST. 'Unless' = if not — signals CONDITION. 'Since' = because — signals CAUSE/EXPLANATION. 'Provided' = on the condition that — signals CONDITION. Only 'since' delivers the causal connection the two clauses need.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Plug each option into the slot. 'ALTHOUGH women are more likely to marry older partners' — would say the 118:100 ratio holds DESPITE women marrying older men; but it's BECAUSE women marry older men that the ratio is so skewed. 'UNLESS women are more likely to marry older partners' — nonsensical; the 'unless' would suggest the ratio only holds if women DON'T marry older men, which is the opposite of the data. 'SINCE women are more likely to marry older partners' — fits: the 25-year-old male surplus exists BECAUSE women look for older partners. 'PROVIDED women are more likely to marry older partners' — would frame women's preference as a precondition for the ratio, which is backward.
steps.title: Plug and test each option

steps.text: The answer is C. The two clauses sit in a cause-effect relationship: women's preference for older partners CAUSES the 25-year-old male surplus. 'Since' (= because) is the only option that carries that meaning.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-034.technique: On cloze conjunctions between two clauses, first identify the LOGICAL relationship — contrast, condition, cause, consequence. Then match the conjunction: 'although'/'though' → contrast; 'if'/'unless'/'provided' → condition; 'since'/'because'/'as' → cause; 'so'/'therefore' → consequence.

distractors.why_tempting: It's easy to read 'or ___' as a binary toggle and reach for 'or not' — meaning 'whether or not they have one'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'A high school certificate or not' is ungrammatical with the indefinite article 'a' attached. The correct English would be 'with or without a certificate'. Step 4 rules this out on grammar grounds.

distractors.why_tempting: Many readers see '174 never-married men for every 100 never-married women' as a sweeping statistic and assume the group must be broad — all education levels at high school AND above.
distractors.why_wrong: The next sentence explicitly anchors the group as 'poorly-educated men' — so the slot must extend DOWN from high school, not up. 'Or more' would capture college graduates too, contradicting the follow-up. Step 3 fixes the direction using the follow-up sentence.

distractors.why_tempting: First instinct on 'or so' is that it's a hedge for approximate certification — 'a high school certificate or thereabouts'.
distractors.why_wrong: 'Or so' attaches to quantities and numbers ('ten or so people'), not to credentials. You wouldn't say 'a master's degree or so'. Step 5 exposes the collocation failure.

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-035.pitfall: Don't pick 'or more' just because the statistic is large. The direction of the slot is fixed by which group the writer is DESCRIBING, not by the size of the number.
host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-035.solution_path: Gap 35 sits in 'high school certificate or ___'. The closing sentence explicitly says 'the difficulty poorly-educated men have finding work' — so the group being measured is those with high-school education OR LESS (worse education). 'Less' is the standard English idiom for 'or below that level'. Answer A.

steps.text: This is a gap-fill (cloze) word question — what's the standard English phrase 'high school certificate or ___'? The slot completes a level-comparison.
steps.title: Understand the gap type

steps.text: Full sentence: 'Among young American adults with a high school certificate or ___, there are 174 never-married men for every 100 never-married women.' Follow-up sentence: 'The difference largely reflects the difficulty poorly-educated men have finding work.' The follow-up names the relevant group as POORLY EDUCATED.
steps.title: Read the sentence around the gap

steps.text: The follow-up sentence is the key — the population in question is described as 'poorly-educated'. So the slot must extend 'high school certificate' DOWNWARD (toward less education), not upward. The relationship needed is 'at this level or below it'.
steps.title: Identify the relationship needed

steps.text: Gloss the candidates. 'Or less' = at that level or below — the standard English idiom for capping an education or income level downward. 'Or not' = ungrammatical here; you'd need 'with a certificate or not' to mean 'whether or not they have one'. 'Or more' = at that level or above — the OPPOSITE direction. 'Or so' = approximately — a quantity hedge that doesn't fit 'a high school certificate or so'.
steps.title: Vocabulary check

steps.text: Plug each option into the slot. 'High school certificate or LESS' — matches the 'poorly-educated' descriptor in the next sentence; idiomatic English. 'High school certificate or NOT' — ungrammatical with the determiner 'a'; would have to be 'with or without a certificate'. 'High school certificate or MORE' — would mean 'at least high school' (including college graduates), but the next sentence specifies POORLY-educated men, so this direction is wrong. 'High school certificate or SO' — 'or so' modifies quantities ('ten or so'), not credentials.
steps.title: Plug and test each option

steps.text: The answer is A. The follow-up sentence's 'poorly-educated men' clinches the direction — the slot must point DOWN the education ladder. 'Or less' is the standard English idiom for that.
steps.title: Conclusion

host-ver2-2019-verb2-ELF-035.technique: On cloze 'X or ___' constructions, look at the surrounding sentence for the direction signal — words like 'poorly', 'limited', 'modest' point DOWN ('or less'); words like 'highly', 'advanced', 'further' point UP ('or more'). 'Or less' and 'or more' are the two real options 90% of the time; 'or not' and 'or so' are usually grammar traps.
